segments.text: "Welcome to Daily Robotics Digest, your daily dose of innovation and insight from the world of robotics.
July 23rd, 2026 - a day that's shaping the future of medicine and robotic capabilities.
Today, we're covering NVIDIA's open-sourced medical physics simulation framework, soft robotic growth without pressure, and more."
From NVIDIA Blog, "NVIDIA Open Sources First GPU-Accelerated Medical Physics Simulation Framework".

NVIDIA has released the first open-source framework that accelerates medical physics simulations on its GPUs, enabling medical robotics developers to create reusable simulation environments for modeling anatomy-device interaction and generating hard-to-capture scenarios.
This framework brings together anatomy and medical device behavior with sensor simulation and robot learning, allowing developers to test in silico before hardware-heavy testing.
By leveraging the power of NVIDIA's GPU acceleration, medical physics simulations can be completed faster, saving time and accelerating innovations to market.
The open-source framework is now available for public use.
The framework was developed through collaboration between researchers, engineers, and industry partners, combining expertise in medical imaging, robotics, and machine learning.
It utilizes a modular approach, allowing developers to easily integrate new components and extend its capabilities.
This innovative technology promises to reduce the time and cost associated with medical physics simulations, enabling faster development of medical robots and treatments.

The release of this framework marks an important milestone for the medical technology industry, which will benefit from accelerated innovation and advancements in medical robotics and treatment technologies.
With this open-source framework, developers can now focus on further refining their innovations, knowing that a reliable simulation environment is available to test and validate their designs.
From Robohub, "Pressure-free growing robots for soft medical robotics"

reports on researchers at the University of Leeds and their collaborators having developed a 1.8 mm soft growing robot that can be steered magnetically, sense its own shape in real time, and operate without internal pressure, reducing friction and potentially improving patient outcomes following minimally invasive procedures.

Researchers created this robot by embedding magnetic particles in silicone to form a "magnetic DNA" pattern that allows for both actuation and sensing, enabling real-time shape control and reduced risk of complications.
By achieving this breakthrough, the team has demonstrated a significant advancement in soft medical robotics, paving the way for more efficient and effective minimally invasive procedures.
This innovation is expected to be further explored and refined in future research.
"Well, that's a wrap for today!
I hope you're recharging your circuits after learning about NVIDIA's groundbreaking move to open source their medical physics simulation framework - talk about a breakthrough in 'health'-tech!
Thanks for tuning in!"
